speaker: interviewer
text: --95. The interview is being conducted in Saddle River, New Jersey. Sulia?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, who came to America as Boris Rubizhewsky and wound up as George B. Roberts, which I changed to Rubin. This is our oldest and first-born, Anita. She'll never forgive me that-- her name is Rita, the middle name. So they used to tease her, Anita Rita Rubin. [LAUGHTER] Now, she's Liebhoff. She was born-- should I tell your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was, she was born August the 11th, 1948, my American citizen, and slept in a dresser drawer. And this is my first-born bored young man, Jeremy Scott Liebhoff. My first one. I waited for him long, but I am the first grandmother, as I said, to see grandchildren. I am very proud of it. I have another one, another daughter-- Ozelle, who is five years younger. She's Ozelle Asher-- was my father's name. She's a shadow after him, Ozelle, Ozelle. And another-- Anita has another son, Adam, who is in Geneva, studying. And I had the youngest one-- all sons-- is Joshua. He is 16 and a half. So I am the lucky one. I have the two daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess you want me to say something now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. By all means.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really haven't given this much thought. I want to tell you that I think I have two wonderful parents. I really never felt-- when they talk about the Holocaust syndrome, children of Holocaust survivors-- I honestly don't feel that I ever felt that way. What I found was that from my parents' experiences, I think they gave me an outlook on life that's wonderful. Because they are survivors. And they've transferred from-- to me, I think, the strength knowing that no matter what life dishes out, I will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So consequently, I find that I roll with the punches, you know. And no matter what happens, I know that I'll be able to deal with it. So it was wonderful. I mean, as I said, my parents I don't ever remember-- until I read your book, Mother-- I never realized how gruesome the experiences were. Seeing it somehow written made it seem that much more alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember the gatherings you had. They had people over all the time in their apartment. And they would sit around the table, and it would be discussed very naturally. We never-- I never looked at it with the horrors that I had to dream about. I never had any nightmares about it. It was almost part of my life. I think it's given me a sense of identity, and how important it is being Jewish. And hopefully my children will carry that on. But in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep my fingers crossed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in this day and age, we never know. Because we don't know what life brings for us. But I know that no matter what happens, I know I will never forget my heritage. I think my son feels the same way. And I think I'm very lucky that I had two parents who gave me a fairly normal upbringing, in spite of the experiences that happened to them. So I really think-- because I know that a lot of other children feel like they're almost beset with a burden, because they are from Holocaust survivors. I think that's about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to keep our house as happy as possible. I used to sing silly songs to them, even to him. You remember [NON-ENGLISH]? Doesn't mean a thing. [LAUGHTER] But I used to sing to them all the time. And I set-- I tried not to burden them with my tragedy. Because she remembers me crying often. And I'd say, it's natural. I miss them. But I tried to give them happiness, some kind of happiness. And I never sold her a Shangri-La. I said, it does not exist. Don't read-- at that time it wasn't it was I said, don't read and don't look at Hollywood. Because nobody smells like a bed of roses all day long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I hope that I leave something, now that I-- she is in the peak of her life. I am at the end of mine. He is in the beginning of his.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that this will transfer something into eternity, that it will hold on what I felt, what we went through, that through us, the next generation will learn. And it's important for me, especially. Because as much as I am trying to preserve it, 52% of my people intermarry and forget, which doesn't have to be forgotten. It could be part of the heritage anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're on. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's, there's not a lot that I can say. I'm separated from it by two degrees. But probably the, the best perspective that I can put on it would be fleeting memories from when I was younger of, of friends of, of my grandparents, who had gone through childhood and the Holocaust with them, in-- 50 years ago. And then to see the bonds that were formed, that could have only been formed by, unfortunately, something so disastrous. And to see, even just as recently as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the past 18 months-- I think it was, couldn't have been more than a year ago that you celebrated your 50th wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago, Jeremy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Time flies to me too, for me too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to see people who, who, you know, you've known since the '30s and '40s talk about, about you and grandpa. And it, it-- that's probably the thing that sticks out in my mind the most. Because there were so many people. I mean, I can only think of very few people who, you know who I have-- would consider to be really good friends and really good people that I'm close to. But there were just so many people that, that you and grandpa were close to, because of this bond that you had formed years ago. And that's probably what's been transcended upon me the most. I can really get a feel for what happened, and why it still can be so fresh in my mind, 50 years later. And will be fresh in my mind for the rest of my life, and hopefully will never ever be forgotten, for generations to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so, Jeremy. I always said, when he was born, and then when he was-- I used to say, you're my ticket into the future, you know? And you said, what's a ticket into the future? I said, it's a very-- a very long trip. It'll take you till the end of your life. [LAUGHTER] But then, I never thought that he retained it. So finally, from grandma's naggings and little things that a grandma says and shouldn't say, something remained. And if 1% remained, I'm ahead of the game. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I'm going to go later.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents. I'll tell you with the titles. This is Dr. Asher Wolozhinksi. And this is my mother. She was a dentist, Dr. Chana Ostashinksy Wolozhinski. She would want her maiden name. They were-- it was approximately-- this is their marriage picture, I got in Leningrad from my cousins. They were approximately married, I believe, 1917. My sister was born in 1919, so this must be 1917. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess I look like mama.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, exactly. I think in Nowogródek. This is my mother in 1923, pregnant with me and blaming me for every pound she gained.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's still blaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mama, I look like her, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Papa. After he became, got the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], he was a rabbi. He went then to a, a theological school. And then, he went-- this is he graduated from teacher's college in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was his second degree, I believe. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know how old he was here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have an idea. You know, I have the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sh. That's Rita in 1927. She was born April the 14th, 1919. She's here eight years old. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And she died when she was 23. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] It was taken in [NON-ENGLISH] Photography, on [NON-ENGLISH] Street in Vilna. This is Rita, age 17. My sister Rita, who won a beauty contest in Kino, which is movies, as Miss Polonia. They didn't know she was Jewish. The name Rita Wolozhinska doesn't sound Jewish. She had everything natural-- auburn hair, almond eyes, the eyebrows-- and she never used lipstick. This is at the same time as my sister's pictures were taken. 1927, [NON-ENGLISH] in Vilna, on [NON-ENGLISH] Street. I loved my outfit. And I especially loved the flowers. They weren't even real, but I wouldn't let them take a picture of me without flowers. I wanted them, I remember. And I clutched on to them. And I got all of those pictures in Leningrad-- now St. Petersburg again-- from my cousins. 1927, it's about December. I finally took off my fur coat and hat. And they put me on a chair. And I was so proud that my sister and I wore the same velvet outfits, but which it doesn't show here-- they were cranberry colored. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] This is 1937, returned from Vilna to Nowogródek. I bought my dog, Alpha, in Druya, on the Latvian border, in a circus. And she was with me till, till the beginning of the end. And I gave her away. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] She was an English fox terrier. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wear the uniform of the high school-- navy coats, seal color, you could not wear anything, the beret, the hat, with the emblem of the school. It usually was an eagle. Each school had a different thing. This is 1938, in my school uniform. But wouldn't you know, I wouldn't let that color go without the pin. I had to adorn it with something and probably was punished for it. Sometimes they suspended you for three days if you wore jewelry in the school. But I wore-- My husband Boris and I in 1946 in Berlin, in Schlachtensee, before we went to America. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] We were full of hope. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoops.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh!

speaker: interviewee
text: Hooray, finally we got the visa to go to the United States. Waited long for a year-- for it, a year and a half. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All the while, we were working and earning our passage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was working for the UNRRA. And Boris was working for the school and organizing all the children. This is across the courthouse, the camp where the last 280 Jews remained. 250 are killed here. It was a mass grave. Mama and Papa are there. Thanks to our good friend, Jack Kagan in London, this was built. Because when I was there in 1969, it was written only peaceful citizens of the town, Nowogródek. Mama and Papa waited here with the rest of them, six hours to die, until they were shot. Many were alive. And the grave shook for, for three days, until everybody died in it. I just hope that they were killed immediately and didn't suffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? Finally, after 50 years and $14, they sent me these two pieces of tin for bravery and recognition for the partisan-- for the partisan fight for the White Russia, Belarus. And the rest is supposed to come. Because my husband had many.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hooray, look at us, look where we arrived. There is, on the right, Anita and Steven. On the left, Ozelle and Stuart, my-- our two giant, four generation Jewish American. Here are [NON-ENGLISH] in the middle, the two midgets, and our interest, our three musketeers. In the middle. Jeremy Scott. To the right of him his brother Adam Craig. To the left, Joshua Reed. I hate to tell you, Joshua Reed now towers over all of them in two years. He's tall. My girls, are our pride and joy. They're nice human beings. Love is nothing-- you love everybody-- but I, I admire them. They turned out to be fabulous human beings. 15 years ago, I finally published all of my diaries-- It was actually meant to be for my daughters and for my grandchildren, to whom I am Mother and Nana. I wanted them to know me as I am, not what they think I am, but what I think I am. And finally, my younger daughter Ozelle typed it. And she decided, Mom, don't ever edit it. Let it be the way you speak. Otherwise, it wouldn't be you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the way the book is written. And I'm proud to say that it's spread now in some colleges. And it's part of the Holocaust education. And if you read this book and sit and listen to me talking, it gives you three credits. It's not a bad deal. Could one imagine, a picture 15 years ago of me. I was 15 years younger, and I still think I looked here old. Can you imagine now? This is on the cover of the book.
